text:
BEAST 2 provides a new, open source, highly flexible and extensible software framework for Bayesian evolutionary analysis. Building on years of experience with the widely used BEAST 1 implementation, BEAST 2 is almost from scratch implemented to address the numerous design constraints that arose as BEAST 1 evolved into a widely used application. In particular, post-implementation extensibility was a major design criterion, and in BEAST 2 this has been realized through a very powerful and flexible package management system, which makes it possible for third party developers to extend BEAST 2 with new analysis priors, models and algorithms in the form of fully executable plug-ins, which do not need to be included in a centralized BEAST 2 software release. An impressive number of very recent publications on BEAST 2 are a direct consequence of this design criterion and include, for example, the implementation of birth-death-sampling tree priors, phylodynamic analysis tools, as well as model averaging over substitution models and over site partitioning models.

Among its many changes, the BEAST 2 source code is made more modular to increase resilience to even more complex kinds of damage, while also increasing speed in some key places, for example by allowing the whole state of the MCMC chain to be read from and written to disk at once, greatly facilitating the process of creating checkpoints in the middle of long runs of MCMC, and also allowing the analysis engine to more fully utilise multi-processor systems and clusters. There is also some reorganization of the interfaces between parts of the code, including to improve BEAST 2's integration with the graphical BEAUti 2. The new modular architecture of the code also allows new modules to extend the GUI provided by BEAUti 2 via a simple and straightforward mechanism, with all parties — author of the new package, users of the package and BEAUti 2 — working with the same (XML) file format as for BEAST trees. In summary, BEAST 2 is available at http://beast2.org and is cross-platform under the GNU LGPL.
